mdx.body:
# Colors and Fonts

CHANGE NIGHT MODE TO DARK MODE

One of the first things I encourage someone to do when starting out a new project is to modify the colors and fonts to match their theme. Plus this provides a quick opportunity to show a couple example of the utility classes in action so let's just started!

The library comes with few default colors, fonts, and modes. I do not expect you to use these, but rather, change them to your theme or at least what looks good in your project. Let's start with the colors, here's what they look like in the \_variables.scss file:

You can change the names to whatever you want, the values to whatever you want, add/remove 'em', etc. The important thing is that they generate utility classes like "text-red" or "bg-blue" for example. If you were to add a color called "primary" or "secondary" for example, if you prefer those kind of names, classes like "text-primary" or "bg-secondary" will be generated.

Additially, different modes cna be added or removed. If a color with the same name is in a different mode, it will be overridden. See more info in the "dark-mode" section. By default I override the 'default' and 'background' color, if you hit the sun/moon icon in the top right you can see the modes toggle and the effects! It's that easy.

One other thing when it comes to colors, is that by default they each get a few shades (except for black and white). You can add/remove/rename/edit these shades in the 'color-modifiers' section, basically the percent values are how much white or black get mixed into each shade. Take note that since 3 colors are overridden in the "dark" mode, they switch to the new colors once that mode is enabled:

It's even possible to add/rename modes if you want, just make sure the pattern follows the existing modes in the $colors map and you add the mode to the $modes list:

Now that you've seen a few examples of the utiltiy classes let's go over fonts. The 'default' fonts are just imported from google fonts and again I'd expect one of the first steps would be to change them to your fonts and import them from either google fonts or your own source:

And if you look in \_variables you can see where teh fonts are named:

So by default these three fonts are used to generate classes using the names "default", "large", and "header". Again the names can be changed to whatever makes sense but going off the default values here's some examples:

I hope these examples have been enough to show you how to get started! Feel free to use the long, exhaustive sidebar menu to see more examples of all the various 'default' classes. Remember, they are meant to be modified and renamed to whatever suits your design or style, and that the library itself is meant to only provide useful, boring stuff, it's not meant to do everything, so that you can write your own scss where you need and have clean style sheets!
